title: Evaluating Parameter Transfer in FALQON Across Graph Families
authors: Alisson dos Passos Fumaco, Marcos Vinicius Reballo, Fernando Augusto Caletti de Barros, Gabriel Fernandes Thomaz, Eduardo I. Duzzioni
abstract: We evaluate FALQON parameter transfer for Max-Cut, transferring sequences from small donors ($n \in \{8,10,12\}$) to 14-node recipients. Using 3-regular and Erdős-Rényi families, we show that transfer success is dictated by the recipient graph, not the donor. Transfer excels for dense recipients -- achieving high approximation ratios regardless of the donor -- but remains challenging in sparse cross-family cases. Crucially, performance is highly resilient to donor size, with 8-node donors matching larger instances. Thus, cheap small graphs can provide robust parameters for larger targets, significantly reducing the measurement overhead of the feedback loop.
summary: This paper investigates how parameters learned by the FALQON quantum optimization algorithm can be reused (transferred) from small test graphs to solve larger, different graph problems like Max-Cut. The key finding is that the target graph's structure, not the source graph's size, determines success. This suggests a pathway to significantly reduce the computational overhead required for scaling quantum optimization algorithms.
main_problem: The paper evaluates the parameter transferability of the FALQON quantum optimization algorithm when applying parameters learned on small graph instances to optimize larger or different graph structures.
main_result: Transfer success is primarily dictated by the structural properties of the recipient graph, showing high resilience to the size of the donor graph, especially for dense target graphs.
method: The authors use an empirical study comparing parameter sequences optimized on small donor graphs (e.g., 8-node) versus larger recipients (14-node) across 3-regular and Erdős–Rényi graph families.
model_or_system: The problem is Max-Cut, formulated as an Ising Hamiltonian solved using the FALQON algorithm, which treats optimization as a closed-loop control problem governed by a time-dependent Schrödinger equation.
key_observables: Approximation ratio (solution cut value relative to the exact optimum), transfer success, and convergence trajectories.
important_parameters: Donor sizes ($n \in \{8, 10, 12\}$), recipient size ($n'=14$), graph density ($p$), and graph families (3-regular, Erdős–Rényi).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes exact state-vector simulation to isolate parameter transferability from hardware noise, and it notes that performance is highly resilient to donor size.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate representative transfer trajectories, showing successful transfer for dense recipients and a clear performance gap for sparse cross-family cases, while also demonstrating parameter overlap across different donor sizes.
paper_structure: The work systematically evaluates parameter transferability across varying problem sizes and Hamiltonian families, comparing results for dense versus sparse graph structures.
why_interesting: The findings on parameter transferability and resilience to donor size are crucial for developing scalable quantum algorithms, suggesting that small, cheap computations can provide robust parameters for much larger, resource-intensive problems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Representative transfer trajectories for dense 14-node Erd˝os–R´enyi recipients (p ∈{0.8, 0.9, 1.0}) from 8-node donor graphs. The transferred mean approximation-ratio curves (blue) exhibit narrow variance and mono- tonic growth, consistently matching or exceeding the donor training trajec- tories (red).

Figure 2. Representative transfer trajectories for sparse 14-node Erd˝os–R´enyi recipients (p ∈{0.2, 0.3, 0.4}) from 3-regular donor graphs of sizes 8, 10, and 12. The transferred curves (blue) remain below the donor references (red) for p = 0.2, with the gap narrowing as recipient density increases.

Figure 3. Comparison of transfer performance across donor sizes for (a) 3- regular and (b) Erd˝os–R´enyi donors (trained at p = 0.5). The substantial overlap in error bars indicates that transfer quality is resilient to donor size, achieving highly competitive and statistically comparable performance even with 8-node donors.
